data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-08T01:37:52.484Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating this .NET Framework code to .NET 8 and need to ensure all Thread.Abort
usage is addressed. Check this code and provide your Thread.Abort migration guidance.

```csharp
public class SimpleWorker
{
    public void RunBatch(List<WorkItem> items)
    {
        var threads = new List<Thread>();

        foreach (var item in items)
        {
            var t = new Thread(() => Process(item));
            t.Start();
            threads.Add(t);
        }

        foreach (var t in threads)
            t.Join();
    }

    private void Process(WorkItem item)
    {
        // simulate work
        Thread.Sleep(100);
        item.Result = ComputeResult(item.Input);
    }
}
```

data.content:
Your code **does not use `Thread.Abort`**, `Thread.Interrupt`, or catch `ThreadAbortException`. It only uses `Thread.Start`, `Thread.Join`, and `Thread.Sleep` — all of which work identically in .NET 8 with no migration needed.

**No changes are required** for Thread.Abort compliance. Your code will run as-is on .NET 8.

That said, modern .NET idiom would favor `Parallel.ForEach` or `Task.Run` over manual `Thread` creation, but that's an optional modernization, not a migration requirement.

The skill description says: "DO NOT USE FOR: code that only uses Thread.Join, Thread.Sleep, or Thread.Start without any abort, interrupt, or ThreadAbortException usage — these APIs work identically in modern .NET and need no migration."

So the skill is explicitly not applicable here. I should tell the user that their code doesn't actually use Thread.Abort and needs no migration for that concern.
